subPages.title: Connect your termux and github and push your code in github
subPages.content:
Pushing directory and updated code to GitHub

Step 1: Generate a Second SSH Key

ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -f ~/.ssh/id_ed25519_profile2 -C "your_second_github_email@example.com"

# replace below for more sophistication

id_ed25519_profile2 => you preferable name of key
e.g. id_ed25519_gitprofile
your_second_github_email@example.com

=============================

Step 2: Add the Key to Your Second GitHub Account

cat ~/.ssh/id_ed25519_profile2.pub

cat ~/.ssh/id_ed25519_vicode-me.pub
Copy the entire text string that appears.Log into your second GitHub profile in your web browser. 
Go to Settings -> SSH and GPG keys -> New SSH key.
Title it something like Termux Profile 2 and paste your key.

=============================

Step 3: Create an SSH Config File (The Magic Trick)


To make Termux automatically know which key to use for which account, 
we will create an SSH config routing file.
Open a new configuration file:
nano ~/.ssh/config
Use code with caution.

Paste the following configuration exactly as shown. This tells Git to use your second key whenever you use the nickname github-profile2:
# Default First Profile (Uses your original key)
Host github.com
    HostName github.com
    User git
    IdentityFile ~/.ssh/id_ed25519

# Second Profile (Uses your new key)
Host github-profile2
    HostName github.com
    User git
    IdentityFile ~/.ssh/id_ed25519_profile2
save via ctrl+o; enter; ctrl+x

==============================

Step 4: Initialize Your Second Local Project

go to project directory via cd

give below git command
git init -b main
----------------------------
Now Set your custom identity for this specific folder only (this ensures your commits show your second profile username and email instead of your first one use below git command
git config user.name "Your Second Profile Name"

=============================

Step 5: Connect and Push to Your Second GitHub Repository
stage 5.1--->

Create a new blank repository on your second GitHub account via your web browser. Do not check the box for a README or .gitignore.
Copy its SSH URL. It will look like this: git@github.com:SECOND_USERNAME/second-repo.git.
When adding the remote link in Termux, modify the domain name to match our SSH config nickname (github-profile2 instead of github.com):
git remote add origin git@github-profile2:SECOND_USERNAME/second-repo.git

stage 5.2--->

git add .
git commit -m "Initial commit for second profile project"
git push -u origin main
when ever new update in code or directory do above and make sure commit it with message [-m] is to add message in commit.
once you push it your code will be in GitHub Repo that you mentioned

id_ed25519_profile2 => you preferable name of key

e.g. id_ed25519_gitprofile

your_second_github_email@example.com

=============================
Step 2: Add the Key to Your Second GitHub Account

cat ~/.ssh/id_ed25519_profile2.pub

Copy the entire text string that appears.Log into your second GitHub profile in your web browser. 
Go to Settings -> SSH and GPG keys -> New SSH key.

Title it something like Termux Profile 2 and paste your key.

=============================
Step 3: Create an SSH Config File (The Magic Trick)

Paste the following configuration exactly as shown. This tells Git to use your second key whenever you use the nickname github-profile2:

# Default First Profile (Uses your original key)
Host github.com
    HostName github.com
    User git
    IdentityFile ~/.ssh/id_ed25519

# Second Profile (Uses your new key)
Host github-profile2
    HostName github.com
    User git
    IdentityFile ~/.ssh/id_ed25519_profile2


save via ctrl+o; enter; ctrl+x

==============================

Step 4: Initialize Your Second Local Project

go to project directory via cd


give below git command

git init -b main

-----
Now Set your custom identity for this specific folder only (this ensures your commits show your second profile username and email instead of your first one

use below git command

git config user.name "Your Second Profile Name"
git config user.email "your_second_github_email@example.com"

=============================

Step 5: Connect and Push to Your Second GitHub Repository
stage 5.1--->

Create a new blank repository on your second GitHub account via your web browser. Do not check the box for a README or .gitignore.

Copy its SSH URL. It will look like this: git@github.com:SECOND_USERNAME/second-repo.git.

When adding the remote link in Termux, modify the domain name to match our SSH config nickname (github-profile2 instead of github.com):


git remote add origin git@github-profile2:SECOND_USERNAME/second-repo.git


stage 5.2--->

git add .
git commit -m "Initial commit for second profile project"
git push -u origin main

----# replace below for more sofastication

id_ed25519_profile2 => you preferable name of key

e.g. id_ed25519_gitprofile

your_second_github_email@example.com

=============================
Step 2: Add the Key to Your Second GitHub Account

cat ~/.ssh/id_ed25519_profile2.pub

Copy the entire text string that appears.Log into your second GitHub profile in your web browser. 
Go to Settings -> SSH and GPG keys -> New SSH key.

Title it something like Termux Profile 2 and paste your key.

=============================
Step 3: Create an SSH Config File (The Magic Trick)

Paste the following configuration exactly as shown. This tells Git to use your second key whenever you use the nickname github-profile2:

# Default First Profile (Uses your original key)
Host github.com
    HostName github.com
    User git
    IdentityFile ~/.ssh/id_ed25519

# Second Profile (Uses your new key)
Host github-profile2
    HostName github.com
    User git
    IdentityFile ~/.ssh/id_ed25519_profile2


save via ctrl+o; enter; ctrl+x

==============================

Step 4: Initialize Your Second Local Project

go to project directory via cd


give below git command

git init -b main

-----
Now Set your custom identity for this specific folder only (this ensures your commits show your second profile username and email instead of your first one

use below git command

git config user.name "Your Second Profile Name"
git config user.email "your_second_github_email@example.com"

=============================

Step 5: Connect and Push to Your Second GitHub Repository
stage 5.1--->

Create a new blank repository on your second GitHub account via your web browser. Do not check the box for a README or .gitignore.

Copy its SSH URL. It will look like this: git@github.com:SECOND_USERNAME/second-repo.git.

When adding the remote link in Termux, modify the domain name to match our SSH config nickname (github-profile2 instead of github.com):


git remote add origin git@github-profile2:SECOND_USERNAME/second-repo.git


stage 5.2--->

git add .
git commit -m "Initial commit for second profile project"
git push -u origin main

e.g. id_ed25519_gitprofile

your_second_github_email@example.com

Step 2: Add the Key to Your Second GitHub Account

cat ~/.ssh/id_ed25519_profile2.pub

Copy the entire text string that appears.Log into your second GitHub profile in your web browser. 
Go to Settings -> SSH and GPG keys -> New SSH key.

Title it something like Termux Profile 2 and paste your key.

=============================
Step 3: Create an SSH Config File (The Magic Trick)

Paste the following configuration exactly as shown. This tells Git to use your second key whenever you use the nickname github-profile2:

# Default First Profile (Uses your original key)
Host github.com
    HostName github.com
    User git
    IdentityFile ~/.ssh/id_ed25519

# Second Profile (Uses your new key)
Host github-profile2
    HostName github.com
    User git
    IdentityFile ~/.ssh/id_ed25519_profile2


save via ctrl+o; enter; ctrl+x

==============================

Step 4: Initialize Your Second Local Project

go to project directory via cd


give below git command

git init -b main

-----
Now Set your custom identity for this specific folder only (this ensures your commits show your second profile username and email instead of your first one

use below git command

git config user.name "Your Second Profile Name"
git config user.email "your_second_github_email@example.com"

=============================

Step 5: Connect and Push to Your Second GitHub Repository
stage 5.1--->

Create a new blank repository on your second GitHub account via your web browser. Do not check the box for a README or .gitignore.

Copy its SSH URL. It will look like this: git@github.com:SECOND_USERNAME/second-repo.git.

When adding the remote link in Termux, modify the domain name to match our SSH config nickname (github-profile2 instead of github.com):


git remote add origin git@github-profile2:SECOND_USERNAME/second-repo.git


stage 5.2--->

git add .
git commit -m "Initial commit for second profile project"
git push -u origin main

    User git
    IdentityFile ~/.ssh/id_ed25519_profile2

go to project directory via cd


give below git command

give below git command

stage 5.2--->

give below git command

git init -b main

-----
Now Set your custom identity for this specific folder only (this ensures your commits show your second profile username and email instead of your first one

use below git command

git config user.name "Your Second Profile Name"
git config user.email "your_second_github_email@example.com"

=============================

Step 5: Connect and Push to Your Second GitHub Repository
stage 5.1--->

Create a new blank repository on your second GitHub account via your web browser. Do not check the box for a README or .gitignore.

Copy its SSH URL. It will look like this: git@github.com:SECOND_USERNAME/second-repo.git.

When adding the remote link in Termux, modify the domain name to match our SSH config nickname (github-profile2 instead of github.com):


git remote add origin git@github-profile2:SECOND_USERNAME/second-repo.git


stage 5.2--->

git add .
git commit -m "Initial commit for second profile project"
git push -u origin main


","newContent":"

Pushing directory and updated code to GitHub


Step 1: Generate a Second SSH Key

ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -f ~/.ssh/id_ed25519_profile2 -C "your_second_github_email@example.com"

# replace below for more sophistication


id_ed25519_profile2 => you preferable name of key

e.g. id_ed25519_gitprofile

your_second_github_email@example.com


=============================

Step 2: Add the Key to Your Second GitHub Account


cat ~/.ssh/id_ed25519_profile2.pub

Copy the entire text string that appears.Log into your second GitHub profile in your web browser. 
Go to Settings -> SSH and GPG keys -> New SSH key.

Title it something like Termux Profile 2 and paste your key.

=============================
Step 3: Create an SSH Config File (The Magic Trick)

Paste the following configuration exactly as shown. This tells Git to use your second key whenever you use the nickname github-profile2:

# Default First Profile (Uses your original key)
Host github.com
    HostName github.com
    User git
    IdentityFile ~/.ssh/id_ed25519

# Second Profile (Uses your new key)
Host github-profile2
    HostName github.com
    User git
    IdentityFile ~/.ssh/id_ed25519_profile2


save via ctrl+o; enter; ctrl+x

==============================

Step 4: Initialize Your Second Local Project


go to project directory via cd

give below git command

git init -b main

-----
Now Set your custom identity for this specific folder only (this ensures your commits show your second profile username and email instead of your first one

use below git command

git config user.name "Your Second Profile Name"

When adding the remote link in Termux, modify the domain name to match our SSH config nickname (github-profile2 instead of github.com):


git remote add origin git@github-profile2:SECOND_USERNAME/second-repo.git


stage 5.2--->


git add .
git commit -m "Initial commit for second profile project"
git push -u origin main


","newContent":"

Pushing directory and updated code to GitHub


Step 1: Generate a Second SSH Key

ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -f ~/.ssh/id_ed25519_profile2 -C "your_second_github_email@example.com"

# replace below for more sophistication


id_ed25519_profile2 => you preferable name of key

e.g. id_ed25519_gitprofile

your_second_github_email@example.com


=============================

Step 2: Add the Key to Your Second GitHub Account


cat ~/.ssh/id_ed25519_profile2.pub

Copy the entire text string that appears.Log into your second GitHub profile in your web browser. 
Go to Settings -> SSH and GPG keys -> New SSH key.

Title it something like Termux Profile 2 and paste your key.

=============================

Step 3: Create an SSH Config File (The Magic Trick)


Paste the following configuration exactly as shown. This tells Git to use your second key whenever you use the nickname github-profile2:

# Default First Profile (Uses your original key)
Host github.com
    HostName github.com
    User git
    IdentityFile ~/.ssh/id_ed25519

# Second Profile (Uses your new key)
Host github-profile2
    HostName github.com
    User git
    IdentityFile ~/.ssh/id_ed25519_profile2


save via ctrl+o; enter; ctrl+x

==============================

Step 4: Initialize Your Second Local Project


go to project directory via cd

give below git command

git init -b main

-----
Now Set your custom identity for this specific folder only (this ensures your commits show your second profile username and email instead of your first one use below git command

git config user.name "Your Second Profile Name"
git config user.email "your_second_github_email@example.com"

=============================

Step 5: Connect and Push to Your Second GitHub Repository
stage 5.1--->


Create a new blank repository on your second GitHub account via your web browser. Do not check the box for a README or .gitignore.

Copy its SSH URL. It will look like this: git@github.com:SECOND_USERNAME/second-repo.git.

When adding the remote link in Termux, modify the domain name to match our SSH config nickname (github-profile2 instead of github.com):


git remote add origin git@github-profile2:SECOND_USERNAME/second-repo.git


stage 5.2--->


git add .
git commit -m "Initial commit for second profile project"
git push -u origin main


"}

Pushing directory and updated code to GitHub


Step 1: Generate a Second SSH Key

ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -f ~/.ssh/id_ed25519_profile2 -C "your_second_github_email@example.com"

# replace below for more sophistication


id_ed25519_profile2 => you preferable name of key

e.g. id_ed25519_gitprofile

your_second_github_email@example.com


=============================

Step 2: Add the Key to Your Second GitHub Account


cat ~/.ssh/id_ed25519_profile2.pub

Copy the entire text string that appears.Log into your second GitHub profile in your web browser. 
Go to Settings -> SSH and GPG keys -> New SSH key.

Title it something like Termux Profile 2 and paste your key.

=============================

Step 3: Create an SSH Config File (The Magic Trick)


Paste the following configuration exactly as shown. This tells Git to use your second key whenever you use the nickname github-profile2:

# Default First Profile (Uses your original key)
Host github.com
    HostName github.com
    User git
    IdentityFile ~/.ssh/id_ed25519

# Second Profile (Uses your new key)
Host github-profile2
    HostName github.com
    User git
    IdentityFile ~/.ssh/id_ed25519_profile2


save via ctrl+o; enter; ctrl+x

==============================

Step 4: Initialize Your Second Local Project


go to project directory via cd

give below git command

git init -b main

-----
Now Set your custom identity for this specific folder only (this ensures your commits show your second profile username and email instead of your first one use below git command

git config user.name "Your Second Profile Name"
git config user.email "your_second_github_email@example.com"

your_second_github_email@example.com


=============================

Step 2: Add the Key to Your Second GitHub Account


cat ~/.ssh/id_ed25519_profile2.pub

Copy the entire text string that appears.Log into your second GitHub profile in your web browser. 
Go to Settings -> SSH and GPG keys -> New SSH key.

Title it something like Termux Profile 2 and paste your key.

=============================

Step 3: Create an SSH Config File (The Magic Trick)


Paste the following configuration exactly as shown. This tells Git to use your second key whenever you use the nickname github-profile2:

# Default First Profile (Uses your original key)
Host github.com
    HostName github.com
    User git
    IdentityFile ~/.ssh/id_ed25519

# Second Profile (Uses your new key)
Host github-profile2
    HostName github.com
    User git
    IdentityFile ~/.ssh/id_ed25519_profile2


save via ctrl+o; enter; ctrl+x

==============================

Step 4: Initialize Your Second Local Project


go to project directory via cd

give below git command

git init -b main

-----
Now Set your custom identity for this specific folder only (this ensures your commits show your second profile username and email instead of your first one use below git command

git config user.name "Your Second Profile Name"
git config user.email "your_second_github_email@example.com"

=============================

Step 5: Connect and Push to Your Second GitHub Repository
stage 5.1--->


Create a new blank repository on your second GitHub account via your web browser. Do not check the box for a README or .gitignore.

Copy its SSH URL. It will look like this: git@github.com:SECOND_USERNAME/second-repo.git.

When adding the remote link in Termux, modify the domain name to match our SSH config nickname (github-profile2 instead of github.com):

git remote add origin git@github-profile2:SECOND_USERNAME/second-repo.git

stage 5.2--->

git add .
git commit -m "Initial commit for second profile project"
git push -u origin main
when ever new update in code or directory do above and make sure commit it with message [-m] is to add message in commit.

Step 1: Generate a Second SSH Key

Step 2: Add the Key to Your Second GitHub Account

Step 2: Add the Key to Your Second GitHub Account


cat ~/.ssh/id_ed25519_profile2.pub

Copy the entire text string that appears.Log into your second GitHub profile in your web browser. 
Go to Settings -> SSH and GPG keys -> New SSH key.

Title it something like Termux Profile 2 and paste your key.

=============================

Step 3: Create an SSH Config File (The Magic Trick)


Paste the following configuration exactly as shown. This tells Git to use your second key whenever you use the nickname github-profile2:

# Default First Profile (Uses your original key)
Host github.com
    HostName github.com
    User git
    IdentityFile ~/.ssh/id_ed25519

# Second Profile (Uses your new key)
Host github-profile2
    HostName github.com
    User git
    IdentityFile ~/.ssh/id_ed25519_profile2


save via ctrl+o; enter; ctrl+x

==============================

Step 4: Initialize Your Second Local Project


go to project directory via cd

give below git command

git init -b main

-----
Now Set your custom identity for this specific folder only (this ensures your commits show your second profile username and email instead of your first one use below git command

git config user.name "Your Second Profile Name"
git config user.email "your_second_github_email@example.com"

=============================

Step 5: Connect and Push to Your Second GitHub Repository
stage 5.1--->


Create a new blank repository on your second GitHub account via your web browser. Do not check the box for a README or .gitignore.
Copy its SSH URL. It will look like this: git@github.com:SECOND_USERNAME/second-repo.git.
When adding the remote link in Termux, modify the domain name to match our SSH config nickname (github-profile2 instead of github.com):
git remote add origin git@github-profile2:SECOND_USERNAME/second-repo.git

stage 5.2--->

git add .
git commit -m "Initial commit for second profile project"
git push -u origin main
when ever new update in code or directory do above and make sure commit it with message [-m] is to add message in commit.

once you push it your code will be in github repo that you mentioned 
"}

give below git command

git init -b main

-----
Now Set your custom identity for this specific folder only (this ensures your commits show your second profile username and email instead of your first one use below git command

git config user.name "Your Second Profile Name"
git config user.email "your_second_github_email@example.com"

=============================

Step 5: Connect and Push to Your Second GitHub Repository
stage 5.1--->


Create a new blank repository on your second GitHub account via your web browser. Do not check the box for a README or .gitignore.
Copy its SSH URL. It will look like this: git@github.com:SECOND_USERNAME/second-repo.git.
When adding the remote link in Termux, modify the domain name to match our SSH config nickname (github-profile2 instead of github.com):
git remote add origin git@github-profile2:SECOND_USERNAME/second-repo.git

stage 5.2--->

git add .
git commit -m "Initial commit for second profile project"
git push -u origin main
when ever new update in code or directory do above and make sure commit it with message [-m] is to add message in commit.

once you push it your code will be in github repo that you mentioned 
","newContent":"

Pushing directory and updated code to GitHub

Step 1: Generate a Second SSH Key

ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -f ~/.ssh/id_ed25519_profile2 -C "your_second_github_email@example.com"

# replace below for more sophistication

id_ed25519_profile2 => you preferable name of key
e.g. id_ed25519_gitprofile
your_second_github_email@example.com

=============================

Step 2: Add the Key to Your Second GitHub Account

cat ~/.ssh/id_ed25519_profile2.pub
Copy the entire text string that appears.Log into your second GitHub profile in your web browser. 
Go to Settings -> SSH and GPG keys -> New SSH key.
Title it something like Termux Profile 2 and paste your key.

=============================

Step 3: Create an SSH Config File (The Magic Trick)


Paste the following configuration exactly as shown. This tells Git to use your second key whenever you use the nickname github-profile2:

# Default First Profile (Uses your original key)
Host github.com
    HostName github.com
    User git
    IdentityFile ~/.ssh/id_ed25519

# Second Profile (Uses your new key)
Host github-profile2
    HostName github.com
    User git
    IdentityFile ~/.ssh/id_ed25519_profile2


save via ctrl+o; enter; ctrl+x

==============================

Step 4: Initialize Your Second Local Project


go to project directory via cd

give below git command

git init -b main

-----
Now Set your custom identity for this specific folder only (this ensures your commits show your second profile username and email instead of your first one use below git command

git config user.name "Your Second Profile Name"
git config user.email "your_second_github_email@example.com"

=============================

Step 5: Connect and Push to Your Second GitHub Repository
stage 5.1--->


Create a new blank repository on your second GitHub account via your web browser. Do not check the box for a README or .gitignore.
Copy its SSH URL. It will look like this: git@github.com:SECOND_USERNAME/second-repo.git.
When adding the remote link in Termux, modify the domain name to match our SSH config nickname (github-profile2 instead of github.com):
git remote add origin git@github-profile2:SECOND_USERNAME/second-repo.git

stage 5.2--->

git add .
git commit -m "Initial commit for second profile project"
git push -u origin main
when ever new update in code or directory do above and make sure commit it with message [-m] is to add message in commit.

once you push it your code will be in github repo that you mentioned 
"}

activities.details:
{"type":"content_change","oldContent":"

Pushing directory and updated code to GitHub

Step 1: Generate a Second SSH Key

ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -f ~/.ssh/id_ed25519_profile2 -C "your_second_github_email@example.com"

# replace below for more sophistication

id_ed25519_profile2 => you preferable name of key
e.g. id_ed25519_gitprofile
your_second_github_email@example.com

=============================

Step 2: Add the Key to Your Second GitHub Account

cat ~/.ssh/id_ed25519_profile2.pub
Copy the entire text string that appears.Log into your second GitHub profile in your web browser. 
Go to Settings -> SSH and GPG keys -> New SSH key.
Title it something like Termux Profile 2 and paste your key.

=============================

Step 3: Create an SSH Config File (The Magic Trick)

Paste the following configuration exactly as shown. This tells Git to use your second key whenever you use the nickname github-profile2:
# Default First Profile (Uses your original key)
Host github.com
    HostName github.com
    User git
    IdentityFile ~/.ssh/id_ed25519

# Second Profile (Uses your new key)
Host github-profile2
    HostName github.com
    User git
    IdentityFile ~/.ssh/id_ed25519_profile2
save via ctrl+o; enter; ctrl+x

==============================

Step 4: Initialize Your Second Local Project


go to project directory via cd

give below git command

git init -b main

-----
Now Set your custom identity for this specific folder only (this ensures your commits show your second profile username and email instead of your first one use below git command

git config user.name "Your Second Profile Name"
git config user.email "your_second_github_email@example.com"

=============================

Step 5: Connect and Push to Your Second GitHub Repository
stage 5.1--->


Create a new blank repository on your second GitHub account via your web browser. Do not check the box for a README or .gitignore.
Copy its SSH URL. It will look like this: git@github.com:SECOND_USERNAME/second-repo.git.
When adding the remote link in Termux, modify the domain name to match our SSH config nickname (github-profile2 instead of github.com):
git remote add origin git@github-profile2:SECOND_USERNAME/second-repo.git

stage 5.2--->

git add .
git commit -m "Initial commit for second profile project"
git push -u origin main
when ever new update in code or directory do above and make sure commit it with message [-m] is to add message in commit.

once you push it your code will be in github repo that you mentioned

    HostName github.com
    User git
    IdentityFile ~/.ssh/id_ed25519

# Second Profile (Uses your new key)
Host github-profile2
    HostName github.com
    User git
    IdentityFile ~/.ssh/id_ed25519_profile2
save via ctrl+o; enter; ctrl+x

==============================

Step 4: Initialize Your Second Local Project

go to project directory via cd

give below git command
git init -b main
-----
Now Set your custom identity for this specific folder only (this ensures your commits show your second profile username and email instead of your first one use below git command
git config user.name "Your Second Profile Name"
git config user.email "your_second_github_email@example.com"
=============================

Step 5: Connect and Push to Your Second GitHub Repository
stage 5.1--->


Create a new blank repository on your second GitHub account via your web browser. Do not check the box for a README or .gitignore.
Copy its SSH URL. It will look like this: git@github.com:SECOND_USERNAME/second-repo.git.
When adding the remote link in Termux, modify the domain name to match our SSH config nickname (github-profile2 instead of github.com):
git remote add origin git@github-profile2:SECOND_USERNAME/second-repo.git

stage 5.2--->

git add .
git commit -m "Initial commit for second profile project"
git push -u origin main
when ever new update in code or directory do above and make sure commit it with message [-m] is to add message in commit.

once you push it your code will be in github repo that you mentioned 
"}

Copy its SSH URL. It will look like this: git@github.com:SECOND_USERNAME/second-repo.git.
When adding the remote link in Termux, modify the domain name to match our SSH config nickname (github-profile2 instead of github.com):
git remote add origin git@github-profile2:SECOND_USERNAME/second-repo.git

stage 5.2--->

git add .
git commit -m "Initial commit for second profile project"
git push -u origin main
when ever new update in code or directory do above and make sure commit it with message [-m] is to add message in commit.
once you push it your code will be in ithub repo that you mentioned 
","newContent":"

Pushing directory and updated code to GitHub

Step 1: Generate a Second SSH Key

ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -f ~/.ssh/id_ed25519_profile2 -C "your_second_github_email@example.com"

# replace below for more sophistication

id_ed25519_profile2 => you preferable name of key
e.g. id_ed25519_gitprofile
your_second_github_email@example.com

=============================

Step 2: Add the Key to Your Second GitHub Account

cat ~/.ssh/id_ed25519_profile2.pub
Copy the entire text string that appears.Log into your second GitHub profile in your web browser. 
Go to Settings -> SSH and GPG keys -> New SSH key.
Title it something like Termux Profile 2 and paste your key.

=============================

Step 3: Create an SSH Config File (The Magic Trick)

Paste the following configuration exactly as shown. This tells Git to use your second key whenever you use the nickname github-profile2:
# Default First Profile (Uses your original key)
Host github.com
    HostName github.com
    User git
    IdentityFile ~/.ssh/id_ed25519

# Second Profile (Uses your new key)
Host github-profile2
    HostName github.com
    User git
    IdentityFile ~/.ssh/id_ed25519_profile2
save via ctrl+o; enter; ctrl+x

==============================

Step 4: Initialize Your Second Local Project

go to project directory via cd

give below git command
git init -b main
-----
Now Set your custom identity for this specific folder only (this ensures your commits show your second profile username and email instead of your first one use below git command
git config user.name "Your Second Profile Name"
git config user.email "your_second_github_email@example.com"

=============================

Step 5: Connect and Push to Your Second GitHub Repository
stage 5.1--->

Create a new blank repository on your second GitHub account via your web browser. Do not check the box for a README or .gitignore.
Copy its SSH URL. It will look like this: git@github.com:SECOND_USERNAME/second-repo.git.
When adding the remote link in Termux, modify the domain name to match our SSH config nickname (github-profile2 instead of github.com):
git remote add origin git@github-profile2:SECOND_USERNAME/second-repo.git

stage 5.2--->

git add .
git commit -m "Initial commit for second profile project"
git push -u origin main
when ever new update in code or directory do above and make sure commit it with message [-m] is to add message in commit.
once you push it your code will be in Github repo that you mentioned 
"}

ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -f ~/.ssh/id_ed25519_profile2 -C "your_second_github_email@example.com"

# replace below for more sophistication

id_ed25519_profile2 => you preferable name of key
e.g. id_ed25519_gitprofile
your_second_github_email@example.com

=============================

Step 2: Add the Key to Your Second GitHub Account

cat ~/.ssh/id_ed25519_profile2.pub
Copy the entire text string that appears.Log into your second GitHub profile in your web browser. 
Go to Settings -> SSH and GPG keys -> New SSH key.
Title it something like Termux Profile 2 and paste your key.

=============================

Step 3: Create an SSH Config File (The Magic Trick)

Paste the following configuration exactly as shown. This tells Git to use your second key whenever you use the nickname github-profile2:
# Default First Profile (Uses your original key)
Host github.com
    HostName github.com
    User git
    IdentityFile ~/.ssh/id_ed25519

# Second Profile (Uses your new key)
Host github-profile2
    HostName github.com
    User git
    IdentityFile ~/.ssh/id_ed25519_profile2
save via ctrl+o; enter; ctrl+x

==============================

Step 4: Initialize Your Second Local Project

go to project directory via cd

give below git command
git init -b main
----------------------------
Now Set your custom identity for this specific folder only (this ensures your commits show your second profile username and email instead of your first one use below git command
git config user.name "Your Second Profile Name"
git config user.email "your_second_github_email@example.com"
=============================

Step 5: Connect and Push to Your Second GitHub Repository
stage 5.1--->

Create a new blank repository on your second GitHub account via your web browser. Do not check the box for a README or .gitignore.
Copy its SSH URL. It will look like this: git@github.com:SECOND_USERNAME/second-repo.git.
When adding the remote link in Termux, modify the domain name to match our SSH config nickname (github-profile2 instead of github.com):
git remote add origin git@github-profile2:SECOND_USERNAME/second-repo.git

stage 5.2--->

git add .
git commit -m "Initial commit for second profile project"
git push -u origin main
when ever new update in code or directory do above and make sure commit it with message [-m] is to add message in commit.
once you push it your code will be in GitHub Repo that you mentioned

Host github-profile2
    HostName github.com
    User git
    IdentityFile ~/.ssh/id_ed25519_profile2
save via ctrl+o; enter; ctrl+x

Step 4: Initialize Your Second Local Project

go to project directory via cd

give below git command
git init -b main
----------------------------
Now Set your custom identity for this specific folder only (this ensures your commits show your second profile username and email instead of your first one use below git command
git config user.name "Your Second Profile Name"
git config user.email "your_second_github_email@example.com"
=============================

Step 5: Connect and Push to Your Second GitHub Repository
stage 5.1--->

Create a new blank repository on your second GitHub account via your web browser. Do not check the box for a README or .gitignore.
Copy its SSH URL. It will look like this: git@github.com:SECOND_USERNAME/second-repo.git.
When adding the remote link in Termux, modify the domain name to match our SSH config nickname (github-profile2 instead of github.com):
git remote add origin git@github-profile2:SECOND_USERNAME/second-repo.git

stage 5.2--->

git add .
git commit -m "Initial commit for second profile project"
git push -u origin main
when ever new update in code or directory do above and make sure commit it with message [-m] is to add message in commit.
once you push it your code will be in GitHub Repo that you mentioned

When adding the remote link in Termux, modify the domain name to match our SSH config nickname (github-profile2 instead of github.com):
git remote add origin git@github-profile2:SECOND_USERNAME/second-repo.git

stage 5.2--->

git add .
git commit -m "Initial commit for second profile project"
git push -u origin main
when ever new update in code or directory do above and make sure commit it with message [-m] is to add message in commit.
once you push it your code will be in GitHub Repo that you mentioned 
","newContent":"

Pushing directory and updated code to GitHub

Step 1: Generate a Second SSH Key

ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -f ~/.ssh/id_ed25519_profile2 -C "your_second_github_email@example.com"

# replace below for more sophistication

id_ed25519_profile2 => you preferable name of key
e.g. id_ed25519_gitprofile
your_second_github_email@example.com

=============================

Step 2: Add the Key to Your Second GitHub Account

cat ~/.ssh/id_ed25519_profile2.pub
Copy the entire text string that appears.Log into your second GitHub profile in your web browser. 
Go to Settings -> SSH and GPG keys -> New SSH key.
Title it something like Termux Profile 2 and paste your key.

=============================

Step 3: Create an SSH Config File (The Magic Trick)

Paste the following configuration exactly as shown. This tells Git to use your second key whenever you use the nickname github-profile2:
# Default First Profile (Uses your original key)
Host github.com
    HostName github.com
    User git
    IdentityFile ~/.ssh/id_ed25519

# Second Profile (Uses your new key)
Host github-profile2
    HostName github.com
    User git
    IdentityFile ~/.ssh/id_ed25519_profile2
save via ctrl+o; enter; ctrl+x

==============================

Step 4: Initialize Your Second Local Project

go to project directory via cd

give below git command
git init -b main
----------------------------
Now Set your custom identity for this specific folder only (this ensures your commits show your second profile username and email instead of your first one use below git command
git config user.name "Your Second Profile Name"
git config user.email "your_second_github_email@example.com"

=============================

Step 5: Connect and Push to Your Second GitHub Repository
stage 5.1--->

Create a new blank repository on your second GitHub account via your web browser. Do not check the box for a README or .gitignore.
Copy its SSH URL. It will look like this: git@github.com:SECOND_USERNAME/second-repo.git.
When adding the remote link in Termux, modify the domain name to match our SSH config nickname (github-profile2 instead of github.com):
git remote add origin git@github-profile2:SECOND_USERNAME/second-repo.git

stage 5.2--->

git add .
git commit -m "Initial commit for second profile project"
git push -u origin main
when ever new update in code or directory do above and make sure commit it with message [-m] is to add message in commit.
once you push it your code will be in GitHub Repo that you mentioned 
"}

ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -f ~/.ssh/id_ed25519_profile2 -C "your_second_github_email@example.com"

ssh-keygen -t ed255

# replace below for more sophistication

id_ed25519_profile2 => you preferable name of key
e.g. id_ed25519_gitprofile
your_second_github_email@example.com

=============================

Step 2: Add the Key to Your Second GitHub Account

cat ~/.ssh/id_ed25519_profile2.pub
Copy the entire text string that appears.Log into your second GitHub profile in your web browser. 
Go to Settings -> SSH and GPG keys -> New SSH key.
Title it something like Termux Profile 2 and paste your key.

=============================

Step 3: Create an SSH Config File (The Magic Trick)

Paste the following configuration exactly as shown. This tells Git to use your second key whenever you use the nickname github-profile2:
# Default First Profile (Uses your original key)
Host github.com
    HostName github.com
    User git
    IdentityFile ~/.ssh/id_ed25519

# Second Profile (Uses your new key)
Host github-profile2
    HostName github.com
    User git
    IdentityFile ~/.ssh/id_ed25519_profile2
save via ctrl+o; enter; ctrl+x

==============================

Step 4: Initialize Your Second Local Project

go to project directory via cd

give below git command
git init -b main
----------------------------
Now Set your custom identity for this specific folder only (this ensures your commits show your second profile username and email instead of your first one use below git command
git config user.name "Your Second Profile Name"
git config user.email "your_second_github_email@example.com"

=============================

Step 5: Connect and Push to Your Second GitHub Repository
stage 5.1--->

Create a new blank repository on your second GitHub account via your web browser. Do not check the box for a README or .gitignore.
Copy its SSH URL. It will look like this: git@github.com:SECOND_USERNAME/second-repo.git.
When adding the remote link in Termux, modify the domain name to match our SSH config nickname (github-profile2 instead of github.com):
git remote add origin git@github-profile2:SECOND_USERNAME/second-repo.git

stage 5.2--->

git add .
git commit -m "Initial commit for second profile project"
git push -u origin main
when ever new update in code or directory do above and make sure commit it with message [-m] is to add message in commit.
once you push it your code will be in GitHub Repo that you mentioned

Copy the entire text string that appears.Log into your second GitHub profile in your web browser. 
Go to Settings -> SSH and GPG keys -> New SSH key.
Title it something like Termux Profile 2 and paste your key.

=============================

Step 3: Create an SSH Config File (The Magic Trick)

Paste the following configuration exactly as shown. This tells Git to use your second key whenever you use the nickname github-profile2:
# Default First Profile (Uses your original key)
Host github.com
    HostName github.com
    User git
    IdentityFile ~/.ssh/id_ed25519

# Second Profile (Uses your new key)
Host github-profile2
    HostName github.com
    User git
    IdentityFile ~/.ssh/id_ed25519_profile2
save via ctrl+o; enter; ctrl+x

==============================

Step 4: Initialize Your Second Local Project

go to project directory via cd

give below git command
git init -b main
----------------------------
Now Set your custom identity for this specific folder only (this ensures your commits show your second profile username and email instead of your first one use below git command
git config user.name "Your Second Profile Name"
git config user.email "your_second_github_email@example.com"

=============================

Step 5: Connect and Push to Your Second GitHub Repository
stage 5.1--->

Create a new blank repository on your second GitHub account via your web browser. Do not check the box for a README or .gitignore.
Copy its SSH URL. It will look like this: git@github.com:SECOND_USERNAME/second-repo.git.
When adding the remote link in Termux, modify the domain name to match our SSH config nickname (github-profile2 instead of github.com):
git remote add origin git@github-profile2:SECOND_USERNAME/second-repo.git

stage 5.2--->

git add .
git commit -m "Initial commit for second profile project"
git push -u origin main
when ever new update in code or directory do above and make sure commit it with message [-m] is to add message in commit.
once you push it your code will be in GitHub Repo that you mentioned 
"}

Step 2: Add the Key to Your Second GitHub Account

cat ~/.ssh/id_ed25519_profile2.pub
Copy the entire text string that appears.Log into your second GitHub profile in your web browser. 
Go to Settings -> SSH and GPG keys -> New SSH key.
Title it something like Termux Profile 2 and paste your key.

=============================

Step 3: Create an SSH Config File (The Magic Trick)

Paste the following configuration exactly as shown. This tells Git to use your second key whenever you use the nickname github-profile2:
# Default First Profile (Uses your original key)
Host github.com
    HostName github.com
    User git
    IdentityFile ~/.ssh/id_ed25519

# Second Profile (Uses your new key)
Host github-profile2
    HostName github.com
    User git
    IdentityFile ~/.ssh/id_ed25519_profile2
save via ctrl+o; enter; ctrl+x

==============================

Step 4: Initialize Your Second Local Project

go to project directory via cd

give below git command
git init -b main
----------------------------
Now Set your custom identity for this specific folder only (this ensures your commits show your second profile username and email instead of your first one use below git command
git config user.name "Your Second Profile Name"
git config user.email "your_second_github_email@example.com"

=============================

Step 5: Connect and Push to Your Second GitHub Repository
stage 5.1--->

Create a new blank repository on your second GitHub account via your web browser. Do not check the box for a README or .gitignore.
Copy its SSH URL. It will look like this: git@github.com:SECOND_USERNAME/second-repo.git.
When adding the remote link in Termux, modify the domain name to match our SSH config nickname (github-profile2 instead of github.com):
git remote add origin git@github-profile2:SECOND_USERNAME/second-repo.git

stage 5.2--->

git add .
git commit -m "Initial commit for second profile project"
git push -u origin main
when ever new update in code or directory do above and make sure commit it with message [-m] is to add message in commit.
once you push it your code will be in GitHub Repo that you mentioned 
"}

{"type":"content_change","oldContent":"

Pushing directory and updated code to GitHub

Step 1: Generate a Second SSH Key

ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -f ~/.ssh/id_ed25519_profile2 -C "your_second_github_email@example.com"

ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -f `~/.ssh/id_ed25519_vicode-me -C "vishalwadekar0987@gmail.com"

# replace below for more sophistication

id_ed25519_profile2 => you preferable name of key
e.g. id_ed25519_gitprofile
your_second_github_email@example.com

=============================

Step 2: Add the Key to Your Second GitHub Account

cat ~/.ssh/id_ed25519_profile2.pub


Copy the entire text string that appears.Log into your second GitHub profile in your web browser. 
Go to Settings -> SSH and GPG keys -> New SSH key.
Title it something like Termux Profile 2 and paste your key.

=============================

Step 3: Create an SSH Config File (The Magic Trick)

Paste the following configuration exactly as shown. This tells Git to use your second key whenever you use the nickname github-profile2:
# Default First Profile (Uses your original key)
Host github.com
    HostName github.com
    User git
    IdentityFile ~/.ssh/id_ed25519

# Second Profile (Uses your new key)
Host github-profile2
    HostName github.com
    User git
    IdentityFile ~/.ssh/id_ed25519_profile2
save via ctrl+o; enter; ctrl+x

==============================

Step 4: Initialize Your Second Local Project

go to project directory via cd

give below git command
git init -b main
----------------------------
Now Set your custom identity for this specific folder only (this ensures your commits show your second profile username and email instead of your first one use below git command
git config user.name "Your Second Profile Name"
git config user.email "your_second_github_email@example.com"

stage 5.2--->

git add .
git commit -m "Initial commit for second profile project"
git push -u origin main
when ever new update in code or directory do above and make sure commit it with message [-m] is to add message in commit.
once you push it your code will be in GitHub Repo that you mentioned

e.g. id_ed25519_gitprofile
your_second_github_email@example.com

=============================

Step 2: Add the Key to Your Second GitHub Account

cat ~/.ssh/id_ed25519_profile2.pub

cat ~/.ssh/id_ed25519_vicode-me.pub
Copy the entire text string that appears.Log into your second GitHub profile in your web browser. 
Go to Settings -> SSH and GPG keys -> New SSH key.
Title it something like Termux Profile 2 and paste your key.

=============================

Step 3: Create an SSH Config File (The Magic Trick)

Paste the following configuration exactly as shown. This tells Git to use your second key whenever you use the nickname github-profile2:
# Default First Profile (Uses your original key)
Host github.com
    HostName github.com
    User git
    IdentityFile ~/.ssh/id_ed25519

# Second Profile (Uses your new key)
Host github-profile2
    HostName github.com
    User git
    IdentityFile ~/.ssh/id_ed25519_profile2
save via ctrl+o; enter; ctrl+x

==============================

Step 4: Initialize Your Second Local Project

go to project directory via cd

give below git command
git init -b main
----------------------------
Now Set your custom identity for this specific folder only (this ensures your commits show your second profile username and email instead of your first one use below git command
git config user.name "Your Second Profile Name"
git config user.email "your_second_github_email@example.com"

=============================

Step 5: Connect and Push to Your Second GitHub Repository
stage 5.1--->

Create a new blank repository on your second GitHub account via your web browser. Do not check the box for a README or .gitignore.
Copy its SSH URL. It will look like this: git@github.com:SECOND_USERNAME/second-repo.git.
When adding the remote link in Termux, modify the domain name to match our SSH config nickname (github-profile2 instead of github.com):
git remote add origin git@github-profile2:SECOND_USERNAME/second-repo.git

stage 5.2--->

git add .
git commit -m "Initial commit for second profile project"
git push -u origin main
when ever new update in code or directory do above and make sure commit it with message [-m] is to add message in commit.
once you push it your code will be in GitHub Repo that you mentioned

Step 3: Create an SSH Config File (The Magic Trick)


To make Termux automatically know which key to use for which account, 
we will create an SSH config routing file.Open a new configuration file:bashnano ~/.ssh/config
Use code with caution.

Paste the following configuration exactly as shown. This tells Git to use your second key whenever you use the nickname github-profile2:
# Default First Profile (Uses your original key)
Host github.com
    HostName github.com
    User git
    IdentityFile ~/.ssh/id_ed25519

# Second Profile (Uses your new key)
Host github-profile2
    HostName github.com
    User git
    IdentityFile ~/.ssh/id_ed25519_profile2
save via ctrl+o; enter; ctrl+x

==============================

Step 4: Initialize Your Second Local Project

go to project directory via cd

give below git command
git init -b main
----------------------------
Now Set your custom identity for this specific folder only (this ensures your commits show your second profile username and email instead of your first one use below git command
git config user.name "Your Second Profile Name"
git config user.email "your_second_github_email@example.com"

=============================

Step 5: Connect and Push to Your Second GitHub Repository
stage 5.1--->

Create a new blank repository on your second GitHub account via your web browser. Do not check the box for a README or .gitignore.
Copy its SSH URL. It will look like this: git@github.com:SECOND_USERNAME/second-repo.git.
When adding the remote link in Termux, modify the domain name to match our SSH config nickname (github-profile2 instead of github.com):
git remote add origin git@github-profile2:SECOND_USERNAME/second-repo.git

stage 5.2--->

git add .
git commit -m "Initial commit for second profile project"
git push -u origin main
when ever new update in code or directory do above and make sure commit it with message [-m] is to add message in commit.
once you push it your code will be in GitHub Repo that you mentioned

id_ed25519_profile2 => you preferable name of key
e.g. id_ed25519_gitprofile
your_second_github_email@example.com

=============================

Step 2: Add the Key to Your Second GitHub Account

cat ~/.ssh/id_ed25519_profile2.pub

cat ~/.ssh/id_ed25519_vicode-me.pub
Copy the entire text string that appears.Log into your second GitHub profile in your web browser. 
Go to Settings -> SSH and GPG keys -> New SSH key.
Title it something like Termux Profile 2 and paste your key.

=============================

Step 3: Create an SSH Config File (The Magic Trick)


To make Termux automatically know which key to use for which account, 
we will create an SSH config routing file.
Open a new configuration file:
nano ~/.ssh/config
Use code with caution.

Paste the following configuration exactly as shown. This tells Git to use your second key whenever you use the nickname github-profile2:
# Default First Profile (Uses your original key)
Host github.com
    HostName github.com
    User git
    IdentityFile ~/.ssh/id_ed25519

# Second Profile (Uses your new key)
Host github-profile2
    HostName github.com
    User git
    IdentityFile ~/.ssh/id_ed25519_profile2
save via ctrl+o; enter; ctrl+x

==============================

Step 4: Initialize Your Second Local Project

go to project directory via cd

give below git command
git init -b main
----------------------------
Now Set your custom identity for this specific folder only (this ensures your commits show your second profile username and email instead of your first one use below git command
git config user.name "Your Second Profile Name"
git config user.email "your_second_github_email@example.com"

=============================

Step 5: Connect and Push to Your Second GitHub Repository
stage 5.1--->

Create a new blank repository on your second GitHub account via your web browser. Do not check the box for a README or .gitignore.
Copy its SSH URL. It will look like this: git@github.com:SECOND_USERNAME/second-repo.git.
When adding the remote link in Termux, modify the domain name to match our SSH config nickname (github-profile2 instead of github.com):
git remote add origin git@github-profile2:SECOND_USERNAME/second-repo.git

stage 5.2--->

git add .
git commit -m "Initial commit for second profile project"
git push -u origin main
when ever new update in code or directory do above and make sure commit it with message [-m] is to add message in commit.
once you push it your code will be in GitHub Repo that you mentioned 
"}

pages.title: Workportal Help Book
pages.content:
README for the WorkPortal repository,

---

# WorkPortal 🚀

**Your Personal Workspace for Notes, Knowledge, and Collaboration**  
A modern, self-hosted knowledge management system that helps you capture, organize, and connect your ideas like never before—all synced across every device on your network.

**Version:** 1.0.0 | **Status:** Active Development | **License:** MIT

---

## ✨ Why WorkPortal?

*   **Knowledge base** – Create and manage rich-text pages, nest sub-pages, and build a personal wiki.
*   **Graph & backlinks** – Visualize connections between your notes and never lose context.
*   **Dashboard & calendar** – Track creation activity and get high-level insights at a glance.
*   **Version history** – Every change is saved; restore any previous version with one click.
*   **Cross‑device sync** – Run the backend once; all devices on your local network stay in sync automatically.
*   **Self‑hosted & private** – No cloud dependencies, no subscription fees. All your data stays on your server.

---

## 🖼️ Showcase

> **Add your visuals here!** Replace the placeholders with your own screenshots or GIFs to give visitors an immediate sense of WorkPortal.

```plaintext
[Screenshot: Editor with formatting toolbar]
[Screenshot: Graph view showing node connections]
[Screenshot: Dashboard with stats and activity]
[Screenshot: Calendar view of page creation dates]
[GIF: Creating a page and watching it sync to a second device]
```

---

## 🛠️ Tech Stack

| Area             | Technology                                                                                                             |
| ---------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| **Frontend**     | React 19, TypeScript, Vite 7, Tailwind CSS 4                                                                           |
| **Editor**       | Custom `contentEditable` rich-text editor with commands (`bold`, `italic`, `underline`, `strikethrough`, lists, etc.) |
| **Backend**      | Node.js (ES modules), Express                                                                                          |
| **Data Storage** | JSON file (`server/state.json`) – simple, portable, zero external dependencies                                        |
| **Sync**         | Optimistic polling with debounced writes and timestamp checks                                                          |
| **UI Components**| Fully custom components built with Tailwind, including toolbars, modals, and panels                                   |

---

## ⚡ Core Features in Detail

### ✍️ Rich Text Editor
- Full formatting toolbar (bold, italic, underline, strikethrough, headings, lists, alignment).
- **Internal links** – link to any page or subpage using a visual modal. Links are stored as special `portal-internal-link` tags with `data-pid` and optional `data-spid` / `data-anchor` attributes.
- **Image upload** – paste or upload images directly; they are base64‑encoded and embedded (no external storage needed).
- **Auto‑save** – content is saved 800 ms after you stop typing. A subtle indicator shows the latest save time.
- **Lock mode** – prevent accidental edits with a per‑page/per‑subpage lock toggle.

### 🌐 Graph View
- Force‑directed graph showing pages, subpages, and the internal links between them.
- **Drag & drop** – reposition nodes to see connections more clearly.
- **Filtering** – search for node labels to highlight specific parts of your graph.
- **Navigation** – click any node to jump directly to that page or subpage.
- Mutual links (bi‑directional) are highlighted with thicker, dashed lines.

### 📊 Dashboard & Analytics
- **Pages & Sub‑pages** – browse all pages, subpages, and pinned items in a searchable list.
- **Global Activity** – view a timeline of all changes (creations, renames, content updates, subpage events) across your entire workspace.
- **Backlinks** – see which pages link to a given page; the dashboard also shows outgoing links.
- **Quick stats** – total pages, subpages, activities, and pinned items are displayed at the top.

### 📅 Calendar View
- Every page records its creation date, which is displayed on a month‑by‑month calendar.
- Click any day to see all pages created on that day; click a page to open it directly.
- Month stats show total pages, active days, and pages created in the selected month.

### 🔗 Backlinks Panel
- Open the panel from the editor to see both incoming and outgoing links for the current page.
- A mini‑graph preview illustrates the connections.
- Click any linked page to navigate instantly.

### ⌨️ Keyboard Shortcuts
- Fully customizable shortcuts (Ctrl+K for search, Ctrl+L for insert link, Ctrl+H for version history, Ctrl+D for dashboard, Ctrl+G for graph, etc.).
- Manage them from the shortcuts modal in the top bar.

### 🔄 Cross‑Device Sync
- One backend instance stores the master `state.json` file.
- All frontend instances poll the backend every 3 seconds; they fetch the full state only when the timestamp changes.
- Writes are debounced and sent to the backend; the backend broadcasts no changes – clients ask for updates.
- This simple, robust approach works instantly on any local network without complex WebSocket setup.

### 🎨 Appearance
- Light and dark themes – toggle via the top bar or your system preference.
- The entire UI adapts seamlessly, including the editor, graph, calendar, and all modals.

### 🧩 Sub‑pages & Trackers
- Every page can have multiple sub‑pages, which behave like independent notes but live under a parent page.
- **Tracker sub‑pages** are automatically created for each page; they log every activity (create, rename, content change, subpage add/delete) in a readable table format.

### 🏷️ Tags
- Add, remove, and filter tags on pages and subpages.
- Tags are currently a simple list (search integration is planned for a future release).

### 📦 Export & Import
- **Export** any page or subpage as Markdown, HTML, JSON, or PDF (via the browser’s print dialog).
- **Full workspace export/import** is available in the Settings modal – you can back up the entire `state.json` or restore a previous backup.

### 📝 Templates
- Six built‑in templates: Meeting Notes, Task List, General Notes, Project Plan, Weekly Review, Bug Report.
- Insert a template directly into the editor to start writing faster.

### 🚀 Production Script (`portal.js`)
- Single‑command management: `node portal.js start --bg` starts the backend in the background and builds the frontend automatically.
- Commands: `start`, `stop`, `restart`, `status`, `logs`.
- Supports `--fg` (foreground) for debugging and `--dev` for development mode with live‑reload.
- The backend serves the built frontend; no separate HTTP server is needed.

---

## 🚀 Getting Started

### Prerequisites
- **Node.js** 20.x or later (v22+ recommended)
- **npm** 9.x or later
- A device on your local network to act as the server (can be a Raspberry Pi, an old laptop, or even your main computer)

### Quick Install

```bash
git clone https://github.com/picoder15/workportal.git
cd workportal
npm install               # install frontend dependencies
cd server && npm install  # install backend dependencies
```

### Running WorkPortal

#### Production Mode (Recommended)

```bash
# From the project root
node portal.js start --bg
```

This command will:
1. Build the frontend (`npm run build`)
2. Start the backend on port **8063** in the background
3. Serve the frontend from the `dist/` folder

Now open a browser on **any device on the same network** and go to `http://<server-ip>:8063`.

#### Development Mode (with hot‑reload)

```bash
node portal.js start --dev --fg
```

This runs both the backend and Vite dev server in the foreground, giving you live‑reload during development.

### Managing the Service

| Command                            | Description                                    |
| ---------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------- |
| `node portal.js start --bg`        | Start in production mode (background)          |
| `node portal.js stop`              | Stop the background process                    |
| `node portal.js restart`           | Restart the background process                 |
| `node portal.js status`            | Check if the service is running                |
| `node portal.js logs`              | Show logs (if any)                             |
| `node portal.js start --fg`        | Start in foreground (logs to console)          |
| `node portal.js start --dev --fg`  | Development mode with live‑reload              |

### Autostart on Termux (Android)

Add this to your `~/.bashrc`:

```bash
WORKPORTAL_DIR="/path/to/workportal"
workportal() {
    (cd "$WORKPORTAL_DIR" && npm run "$1")
}
if [ -f "$WORKPORTAL_DIR/.portal.pid" ] && kill -0 $(cat "$WORKPORTAL_DIR/.portal.pid") 2>/dev/null; then
    : # Already running
else
    (cd "$WORKPORTAL_DIR" && npm run start > /dev/null 2>&1 &)
fi
```

Then use `workportal start`, `workportal stop`, etc., from anywhere.

---

## 📁 Project Structure

```
workportal/
├── server/                 # Backend (Node.js + Express)
│   ├── index.js            # Main server file (API + static hosting)
│   ├── state.json          # Workspace data (auto‑created)
│   └── package.json
├── src/                    # Frontend (React + TypeScript)
│   ├── components/         # All UI components
│   ├── context/            # PortalContext (state management + sync)
│   ├── types/              # TypeScript definitions
│   ├── utils/              # Helper functions
│   ├── App.tsx
│   ├── main.tsx
│   └── index.css
├── portal.js               # Unified management script
├── index.html
├── package.json
├── vite.config.ts
└── tsconfig.json
```

---

## 🔌 API Endpoints

| Method | Endpoint                | Description                         |
|--------|-------------------------|-------------------------------------|
| GET    | `/api/state`            | Get the full workspace state        |
| PUT    | `/api/state`            | Overwrite the workspace state       |
| GET    | `/api/state/timestamp`  | Get the last modification timestamp |

All other routes serve the built frontend (single‑page app fallback).

---

## 📝 Configuration

- **Port**: Default is `8063`. Change it by setting the `PORT` environment variable or passing `--port <number>` to `portal.js`.
- **State file location**: `server/state.json`. You can back this file up, move it to another server, or share it between instances.
- **Network access**: The server binds to `0.0.0.0` so it is reachable from any device on your local network.

---

## 🧪 Testing & Debugging

- **Foreground mode** (`--fg`) shows all backend logs in the terminal – useful for debugging sync issues.
- **Check sync**: Open WorkPortal on two different devices, create a page on one, and after ~3 seconds it should appear on the other.
- **View state file**: `cat server/state.json | jq '.pages | length'` shows how many pages are stored.
- **Monitor polling**: Open your browser’s DevTools → Network tab and filter for `/api/state/timestamp`.

{"type":"content_change","oldContent":"","newContent":"README for the WorkPortal repository,

---

# WorkPortal 🚀

**Your Personal Workspace for Notes, Knowledge, and Collaboration**  
A modern, self-hosted knowledge management system that helps you capture, organize, and connect your ideas like never before—all synced across every device on your network.

**Version:** 1.0.0 | **Status:** Active Development | **License:** MIT

---

## ✨ Why WorkPortal?

*   **Knowledge base** – Create and manage rich-text pages, nest sub-pages, and build a personal wiki.
*   **Graph & backlinks** – Visualize connections between your notes and never lose context.
*   **Dashboard & calendar** – Track creation activity and get high-level insights at a glance.
*   **Version history** – Every change is saved; restore any previous version with one click.
*   **Cross‑device sync** – Run the backend once; all devices on your local network stay in sync automatically.
*   **Self‑hosted & private** – No cloud dependencies, no subscription fees. All your data stays on your server.

---

## 🖼️ Showcase

> **Add your visuals here!** Replace the placeholders with your own screenshots or GIFs to give visitors an immediate sense of WorkPortal.

```plaintext
[Screenshot: Editor with formatting toolbar]
[Screenshot: Graph view showing node connections]
[Screenshot: Dashboard with stats and activity]
[Screenshot: Calendar view of page creation dates]
[GIF: Creating a page and watching it sync to a second device]
```

---

## 🛠️ Tech Stack

| Area             | Technology                                                                                                             |
| ---------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| **Frontend**     | React 19, TypeScript, Vite 7, Tailwind CSS 4                                                                           |
| **Editor**       | Custom `contentEditable` rich-text editor with commands (`bold`, `italic`, `underline`, `strikethrough`, lists, etc.) |
| **Backend**      | Node.js (ES modules), Express                                                                                          |
| **Data Storage** | JSON file (`server/state.json`) – simple, portable, zero external dependencies                                        |
| **Sync**         | Optimistic polling with debounced writes and timestamp checks                                                          |
| **UI Components**| Fully custom components built with Tailwind, including toolbars, modals, and panels                                   |

---

## ⚡ Core Features in Detail

### ✍️ Rich Text Editor
- Full formatting toolbar (bold, italic, underline, strikethrough, headings, lists, alignment).
- **Internal links** – link to any page or subpage using a visual modal. Links are stored as special `portal-internal-link` tags with `data-pid` and optional `data-spid` / `data-anchor` attributes.
- **Image upload** – paste or upload images directly; they are base64‑encoded and embedded (no external storage needed).
- **Auto‑save** – content is saved 800 ms after you stop typing. A subtle indicator shows the latest save time.
- **Lock mode** – prevent accidental edits with a per‑page/per‑subpage lock toggle.

### 🌐 Graph View
- Force‑directed graph showing pages, subpages, and the internal links between them.
- **Drag & drop** – reposition nodes to see connections more clearly.
- **Filtering** – search for node labels to highlight specific parts of your graph.
- **Navigation** – click any node to jump directly to that page or subpage.
- Mutual links (bi‑directional) are highlighted with thicker, dashed lines.

### 📊 Dashboard & Analytics
- **Pages & Sub‑pages** – browse all pages, subpages, and pinned items in a searchable list.
- **Global Activity** – view a timeline of all changes (creations, renames, content updates, subpage events) across your entire workspace.
- **Backlinks** – see which pages link to a given page; the dashboard also shows outgoing links.
- **Quick stats** – total pages, subpages, activities, and pinned items are displayed at the top.

### 📅 Calendar View
- Every page records its creation date, which is displayed on a month‑by‑month calendar.
- Click any day to see all pages created on that day; click a page to open it directly.
- Month stats show total pages, active days, and pages created in the selected month.

### 🔗 Backlinks Panel
- Open the panel from the editor to see both incoming and outgoing links for the current page.
- A mini‑graph preview illustrates the connections.
- Click any linked page to navigate instantly.

### ⌨️ Keyboard Shortcuts
- Fully customizable shortcuts (Ctrl+K for search, Ctrl+L for insert link, Ctrl+H for version history, Ctrl+D for dashboard, Ctrl+G for graph, etc.).
- Manage them from the shortcuts modal in the top bar.

### 🔄 Cross‑Device Sync
- One backend instance stores the master `state.json` file.
- All frontend instances poll the backend every 3 seconds; they fetch the full state only when the timestamp changes.
- Writes are debounced and sent to the backend; the backend broadcasts no changes – clients ask for updates.
- This simple, robust approach works instantly on any local network without complex WebSocket setup.

### 🎨 Appearance
- Light and dark themes – toggle via the top bar or your system preference.
- The entire UI adapts seamlessly, including the editor, graph, calendar, and all modals.

### 🧩 Sub‑pages & Trackers
- Every page can have multiple sub‑pages, which behave like independent notes but live under a parent page.
- **Tracker sub‑pages** are automatically created for each page; they log every activity (create, rename, content change, subpage add/delete) in a readable table format.

### 🏷️ Tags
- Add, remove, and filter tags on pages and subpages.
- Tags are currently a simple list (search integration is planned for a future release).

### 📦 Export & Import
- **Export** any page or subpage as Markdown, HTML, JSON, or PDF (via the browser’s print dialog).
- **Full workspace export/import** is available in the Settings modal – you can back up the entire `state.json` or restore a previous backup.

### 📝 Templates
- Six built‑in templates: Meeting Notes, Task List, General Notes, Project Plan, Weekly Review, Bug Report.
- Insert a template directly into the editor to start writing faster.

### 🚀 Production Script (`portal.js`)
- Single‑command management: `node portal.js start --bg` starts the backend in the background and builds the frontend automatically.
- Commands: `start`, `stop`, `restart`, `status`, `logs`.
- Supports `--fg` (foreground) for debugging and `--dev` for development mode with live‑reload.
- The backend serves the built frontend; no separate HTTP server is needed.

---

## 🚀 Getting Started

### Prerequisites
- **Node.js** 20.x or later (v22+ recommended)
- **npm** 9.x or later
- A device on your local network to act as the server (can be a Raspberry Pi, an old laptop, or even your main computer)

### Quick Install

```bash
git clone https://github.com/picoder15/workportal.git
cd workportal
npm install               # install frontend dependencies
cd server && npm install  # install backend dependencies
```

### Running WorkPortal

#### Production Mode (Recommended)

```bash
# From the project root
node portal.js start --bg
```

This command will:
1. Build the frontend (`npm run build`)
2. Start the backend on port **8063** in the background
3. Serve the frontend from the `dist/` folder

Now open a browser on **any device on the same network** and go to `http://<server-ip>:8063`.

#### Development Mode (with hot‑reload)

```bash
node portal.js start --dev --fg
```

This runs both the backend and Vite dev server in the foreground, giving you live‑reload during development.

### Managing the Service

| Command                            | Description                                    |
| ---------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------- |
| `node portal.js start --bg`        | Start in production mode (background)          |
| `node portal.js stop`              | Stop the background process                    |
| `node portal.js restart`           | Restart the background process                 |
| `node portal.js status`            | Check if the service is running                |
| `node portal.js logs`              | Show logs (if any)                             |
| `node portal.js start --fg`        | Start in foreground (logs to console)          |
| `node portal.js start --dev --fg`  | Development mode with live‑reload              |

### Autostart on Termux (Android)

Add this to your `~/.bashrc`:

```bash
WORKPORTAL_DIR=\"/path/to/workportal\"
workportal() {
    (cd \"$WORKPORTAL_DIR\" && npm run \"$1\")
}
if [ -f \"$WORKPORTAL_DIR/.portal.pid\" ] && kill -0 $(cat \"$WORKPORTAL_DIR/.portal.pid\") 2>/dev/null; then
    : # Already running
else
    (cd \"$WORKPORTAL_DIR\" && npm run start > /dev/null 2>&1 &)
fi
```

Then use `workportal start`, `workportal stop`, etc., from anywhere.

---

workportal/
├── server/                 # Backend (Node.js + Express)

- **State file location**: `server/state.json`. You can back this file up, move it to another server, or share it between instances.

Side-bar features Sub Page 1 Missing feature Make sure you invent each feature one at a time or not more than 2 

Portal Features


0) Need a more feature is that page, subpage and its sub-subpage if user want then create a array of subpage but it need to properly structure
1) there is no any feature available to see this article in sidebar or new page
2) Need to export the article to .txt file --- its missing
3) what to do with the tags - how to check which article has same tags
4) Graph need to more featureful
5) symbols used in the portal is look like a very chat gpt generated, it need to change to symbols style which are used in editor tool bar.
8) make a page subpage, or make a subpage page, but it need to track properly in history
9) button to open the portal in full screen of browser
10) show total count of subpage in front of main-page name in side bar

Editor-----

1) need to add a checkbox feature in tool bar so we can add a checkbox feature here
2) Need a feature of highlight the text 
3) Add code block in the article and make it lock and copy it via copy button in front of its
4) collapse the headings wise content in the 

Security features

1) Login for portal
2) need a feature visibility lock of article - by clicking on the visibility lock we can hide the context in the article if we again click on visibility lock it will , ask password root password or sudo password to unlock the article content and make it visible , 
3) cyper the article even if the article is visible if we make the article cyper lock so no user will read it until and unless cyper lock get removed, which can be unlock by a cyper password. need to thing on it very precisely because the feature is now dealing with the content of article.
4) Accidental deletion protection of page/subpage/array
5) add a shortcut for locking the page to viewonly

subPages.title: # Initialize default configuration
subPages.content:
#!/bin/bash

# Termux Profile Backup Script
# Version 9.1
# Author: Vish-l
# Last Modified: 2025-07-09

# Initialize default configuration
DEFAULT_BACKUP_DIR="/data/data/com.termux/files/home/termux_backup"
DEFAULT_LOG_DIR="/data/data/com.termux/files/home/logs/termux_profile_backup"
DEFAULT_LOG_ARCHIVE_AFTER=14 # Days before archiving logs
DEFAULT_ARCHIVE_DELETE_AFTER=90 # Days before deleting archives
DEFAULT_NOTIFICATIONS=true
DEFAULT_BACKUP_FORMAT="tar.gz"
DEFAULT_IGNORE_FILE="/data/data/com.termux/files/home/.termux_backup_ignore"
DEFAULT_CLEAN_OLD_DAYS=30 # Default days for cleaning old backups
DEFAULT_REPORT_RETENTION=7 # Days to keep reports
DEFAULT_CRON_LOG_RETENTION=30 # Days to keep cron logs
DEFAULT_BACKUP_PERMISSION="600" # Default backup file permission

CONFIG_FILE="/data/data/com.termux/files/home/.termux_backup_config"

subPages.title: # Load or initialize configuration
subPages.content:
# Load or initialize configuration
load_config() {
if [ -f "$CONFIG_FILE" ]; then
# Safely source config file
if ! source "$CONFIG_FILE"; then
echo "Error loading config file. Using defaults."
initialize_default_config
fi
else
initialize_default_config
fi
# Ensure dependent variables are set
: ${USER_HELP_FILE:="$LOG_DIR/user_define_help.txt"}
: ${REPORT_DIR:="$LOG_DIR/reports"}
: ${REPORT_FILE_PREFIX:="$LOG_DIR/backup_report"}
: ${CRON_LOG_RETENTION:=$DEFAULT_CRON_LOG_RETENTION}
: ${CRON_LOG_DIR:="$LOG_DIR/cron_logs"}
: ${BACKUP_FILE_PERMISSION:=$DEFAULT_BACKUP_PERMISSION}
}

activities.description: Sub page "# Load or initialize configuration" content updated
activities.details:
{"type":"content_change","oldContent":"
# Load or initialize configuration
load_config() {
if [ -f \"$CONFIG_FILE\" ]; then
# Safely source config file
if ! source \"$CONFIG_FILE\"; then
echo \"Error loading config file. Using defaults.\"
initialize_default_config
fi
else
initialize_default_config
fi
# Ensure dependent variables are set
: ${USER_HELP_FILE:=\"$LOG_DIR/user_define_help.txt\"}
: ${REPORT_DIR:=\"$LOG_DIR/reports\"}
: ${REPORT_FILE_PREFIX:=\"$LOG_DIR/backup_report\"}
: ${CRON_LOG_RETENTION:=$DEFAULT_CRON_LOG_RETENTION}
: ${CRON_LOG_DIR:=\"$LOG_DIR/cron_logs\"}
: ${BACKUP_FILE_PERMISSION:=$DEFAULT_BACKUP_PERMISSION}
}

{"type":"content_change","oldContent":"
# Load or initialize configuration
load_config() {
if [ -f \"$CONFIG_FILE\" ]; then
# Safely source config file
if ! source \"$CONFIG_FILE\"; then
echo \"Error loading config file. Using defaults.\"
initialize_default_config
fi
else
initialize_default_config
fi
# Ensure dependent variables are set
: ${USER_HELP_FILE:=\"$LOG_DIR/user_define_help.txt\"}
: ${REPORT_DIR:=\"$LOG_DIR/reports\"}
: ${REPORT_FILE_PREFIX:=\"$LOG_DIR/backup_report\"}
: ${CRON_LOG_RETENTION:=$DEFAULT_CRON_LOG_RETENTION}
: ${CRON_LOG_DIR:=\"$LOG_DIR/cron_logs\"}
: ${BACKUP_FILE_PERMISSION:=$DEFAULT_BACKUP_PERMISSION}
}


if [ -f \"$config_file\" ]; then
if source \"$config_file\"; then
initialize_default_config
else
echo
